---
title: "Wooldridge EACSPD -- Ch. 15: Discrete response models (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Stata examples reproduced from [UCLA OARC — EACSPD Chapter 15](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/examples/eacspd/econometric-analysis-of-cross-section-and-panel-data-by-jeffrey-m-wooldridgechapter-15-discrete-response-models/).

Textbook: Wooldridge, J. M., *Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel Data*, MIT Press.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

# Data

```{julia}
mroz    = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("mroz"))
keane   = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("keane"))
pension = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("pension"))
(size(mroz), size(keane), size(pension))
```

## Example 15.1 (mroz.dta)

The linear probability model for labour-force participation (p. 455). The UCLA
page notes that Stata's robust standard errors carry a finite-sample
correction, so they differ slightly from the book's.

```{julia}
#=
use mroz, clear

reg inlf nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =     753
-------------+------------------------------           F(  7,   745) =   38.22
       Model |  48.8080578     7  6.97257969           Prob > F      =  0.0000
    Residual |  135.919698   745  .182442547           R-squared     =  0.2642
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.2573
       Total |  184.727756   752  .245648611           Root MSE      =  .42713

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        inlf |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    nwifeinc |  -.0034052   .0014485    -2.35   0.019    -.0062488   -.0005616
        educ |   .0379953    .007376     5.15   0.000      .023515    .0524756
       exper |   .0394924   .0056727     6.96   0.000     .0283561    .0506287
     expersq |  -.0005963   .0001848    -3.23   0.001    -.0009591   -.0002335
         age |  -.0160908   .0024847    -6.48   0.000    -.0209686    -.011213
     kidslt6 |  -.2618105   .0335058    -7.81   0.000    -.3275875   -.1960335
     kidsge6 |   .0130122    .013196     0.99   0.324    -.0128935    .0389179
       _cons |   .5855192   .154178      3.80   0.000     .2828442    .8881943
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

reg inlf nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6, robust

Regression with robust standard errors                 Number of obs =     753
                                                       F(  7,   745) =   62.48
                                                       Prob > F      =  0.0000
                                                       R-squared     =  0.2642
                                                       Root MSE      =  .42713

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
             |               Robust
        inlf |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    nwifeinc |  -.0034052   .0015249    -2.23   0.026    -.0063988   -.0004115
        educ |   .0379953    .007266     5.23   0.000      .023731    .0522596
       exper |   .0394924     .00581     6.80   0.000     .0280864    .0508983
     expersq |  -.0005963     .00019    -3.14   0.002    -.0009693   -.0002233
         age |  -.0160908    .002399    -6.71   0.000    -.0208004   -.0113812
     kidslt6 |  -.2618105   .0317832    -8.24   0.000    -.3242058   -.1994152
     kidsge6 |   .0130122   .0135329     0.96   0.337     -.013555    .0395795
       _cons |   .5855192   .1522599     3.85   0.000     .2866098    .8844287
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
XLPM = [:nwifeinc, :educ, :exper, :expersq, :age, :kidslt6, :kidsge6]
lpm_ols = stata_regress(mroz; y = :inlf, x = XLPM)
```

```{julia}
lpm_rob = stata_regress(mroz; y = :inlf, x = XLPM, vce = :robust)
```

**Comparison.** Both regressions reproduce the published Stata output digit for
digit: all eight coefficients, both sets of standard errors, `F(7,745) = 38.22`
(OLS) and `62.48` (robust), `R-squared = 0.2642`, `Root MSE = .42713`.
`FixedEffectModels` applies the same `n/(n−k)` finite-sample correction to the
HC1 robust variance that Stata does, which is why the robust SEs agree to all
seven printed digits.

## Example 15.2 (mroz.dta)

LPM vs. logit vs. probit, with `estat classification` after each binary model
(p. 468).

```{julia}
#=
reg inlf nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =     753
-------------+------------------------------           F(  7,   745) =   38.22
       Model |  48.8080578     7  6.97257969           Prob > F      =  0.0000
    Residual |  135.919698   745  .182442547           R-squared     =  0.2642
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.2573
       Total |  184.727756   752  .245648611           Root MSE      =  .42713

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        inlf |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    nwifeinc |  -.0034052   .0014485    -2.35   0.019    -.0062488   -.0005616
        educ |   .0379953    .007376     5.15   0.000      .023515    .0524756
       exper |   .0394924   .0056727     6.96   0.000     .0283561    .0506287
     expersq |  -.0005963   .0001848    -3.23   0.001    -.0009591   -.0002335
         age |  -.0160908   .0024847    -6.48   0.000    -.0209686    -.011213
     kidslt6 |  -.2618105   .0335058    -7.81   0.000    -.3275875   -.1960335
     kidsge6 |   .0130122    .013196     0.99   0.324    -.0128935    .0389179
       _cons |   .5855192   .154178      3.80   0.000     .2828442    .8881943
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

predict lpm
gen lpm_c = (lpm>=.5)
tab inlf lpm_c

  =1 if in |
 lab frce, |         lpm_c
      1975 |         0          1 |     Total
-----------+----------------------+----------
         0 |       203        122 |       325
         1 |        78        350 |       428
-----------+----------------------+----------
     Total |       281        472 |       753

di (203+350)/753
.73439575

logit inlf nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6

Logit estimates                                   Number of obs   =        753
                                                  LR chi2(7)      =     226.22
                                                  Prob > chi2     =     0.0000
Log likelihood = -401.76515                       Pseudo R2       =     0.2197

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        inlf |      Coef.   Std. Err.      z    P>|z|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    nwifeinc |  -.0213452   .0084214    -2.53   0.011    -.0378509   -.0048394
        educ |   .2211704   .0434396     5.09   0.000     .1360303    .3063105
       exper |   .2058695   .0320569     6.42   0.000     .1430391    .2686999
     expersq |  -.0031541   .0010161    -3.10   0.002    -.0051456   -.0011626
         age |  -.0880244    .014573    -6.04   0.000     -.116587   -.0594618
     kidslt6 |  -1.443354   .2035849    -7.09   0.000    -1.842373   -1.044335
     kidsge6 |   .0601122   .0747897     0.80   0.422     -.086473    .2066974
       _cons |   .4254524   .8603696     0.49   0.621    -1.260841    2.111746
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

* Stata 8 code.
lstat

* Stata 9 code and output.
estat classification

Logistic model for inlf

              -------- True --------
Classified |         D            ~D  |      Total
-----------+--------------------------+-----------
     +     |       347           118  |        465
     -     |        81           207  |        288
-----------+--------------------------+-----------
   Total   |       428           325  |        753

Classified + if predicted Pr(D) >= .5
True D defined as inlf != 0
--------------------------------------------------
Sensitivity                     Pr( +| D)   81.07%
Specificity                     Pr( -|~D)   63.69%
Positive predictive value       Pr( D| +)   74.62%
Negative predictive value       Pr(~D| -)   71.88%
--------------------------------------------------
False + rate for true ~D        Pr( +|~D)   36.31%
False - rate for true D         Pr( -| D)   18.93%
False + rate for classified +   Pr(~D| +)   25.38%
False - rate for classified -   Pr( D| -)   28.13%
--------------------------------------------------
Correctly classified                        73.57%
--------------------------------------------------

probit inlf nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6

Probit estimates                                  Number of obs   =        753
                                                  LR chi2(7)      =     227.14
                                                  Prob > chi2     =     0.0000
Log likelihood = -401.30219                       Pseudo R2       =     0.2206

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        inlf |      Coef.   Std. Err.      z    P>|z|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    nwifeinc |  -.0120237   .0048398    -2.48   0.013    -.0215096   -.0025378
        educ |   .1309047   .0252542     5.18   0.000     .0814074     .180402
       exper |   .1233476   .0187164     6.59   0.000     .0866641    .1600311
     expersq |  -.0018871      .0006    -3.15   0.002     -.003063   -.0007111
         age |  -.0528527   .0084772    -6.23   0.000    -.0694678   -.0362376
     kidslt6 |  -.8683285   .1185223    -7.33   0.000    -1.100628    -.636029
     kidsge6 |    .036005   .0434768     0.83   0.408     -.049208    .1212179
       _cons |   .2700768    .508593     0.53   0.595    -.7267473    1.266901
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

* Stata 8 code.
lstat

* Stata 9 code and output.
estat classification

Probit model for inlf

              -------- True --------
Classified |         D            ~D  |      Total
-----------+--------------------------+-----------
     +     |       348           120  |        468
     -     |        80           205  |        285
-----------+--------------------------+-----------
   Total   |       428           325  |        753

Classified + if predicted Pr(D) >= .5
True D defined as inlf != 0
--------------------------------------------------
Sensitivity                     Pr( +| D)   81.31%
Specificity                     Pr( -|~D)   63.08%
Positive predictive value       Pr( D| +)   74.36%
Negative predictive value       Pr(~D| -)   71.93%
--------------------------------------------------
False + rate for true ~D        Pr( +|~D)   36.92%
False - rate for true D         Pr( -| D)   18.69%
False + rate for classified +   Pr(~D| +)   25.64%
False - rate for classified -   Pr( D| -)   28.07%
--------------------------------------------------
Correctly classified                        73.44%
--------------------------------------------------
=#
```

### LPM classification table (`predict lpm` / `tab inlf lpm_c`)

```{julia}
lpm_hat = FixedEffectModels.predict(lpm_ols, mroz)
lpm_c   = Int.(lpm_hat .>= 0.5)
inlf    = Int.(mroz.inlf)

tab = [sum((inlf .== i) .& (lpm_c .== j)) for i in 0:1, j in 0:1]
Printf.@printf("        lpm_c=0  lpm_c=1   Total\n")
for i in 1:2
    Printf.@printf("inlf=%d  %7d  %7d %7d\n", i - 1, tab[i, 1], tab[i, 2], sum(tab[i, :]))
end
Printf.@printf("Total   %7d  %7d %7d\n", sum(tab[:, 1]), sum(tab[:, 2]), sum(tab))
Printf.@printf("\ncorrectly classified = %.8f\n", (tab[1, 1] + tab[2, 2]) / sum(tab))
```

**Comparison.** The cross-tabulation is identical to Stata's:
203 / 122 / 78 / 350, totals 281 / 472 / 753, and the fraction correctly
classified is `.73439575`, exactly the value Stata's `di (203+350)/753` prints.

### Logit

```{julia}
f_inlf = StatsModels.@formula(inlf ~ nwifeinc + educ + exper + expersq +
                                     age + kidslt6 + kidsge6)
lg = stata_logit(mroz, f_inlf)
```

```{julia}
stata_estat_classification(lg.model, mroz; depvar = "inlf")
```

### Probit

```{julia}
pb = stata_probit(mroz, f_inlf)
```

```{julia}
stata_estat_classification(pb.model, mroz; depvar = "inlf")
```

**Comparison.** Logit and probit coefficients, the log-likelihoods
(−401.76515 and −401.30219), `LR chi2(7)` (226.22 and 227.14) and the pseudo
R² (0.2197 / 0.2206) all reproduce the published values. Both classification
blocks match Stata cell for cell — logit 347 / 118 / 81 / 207 with **73.57%**
correctly classified, probit 348 / 120 / 80 / 205 with **73.44%** — including
every sensitivity/specificity/PPV/NPV percentage (the single 0.01-point
difference, logit "False − rate for classified −" 28.12% vs Stata's 28.13%, is
a rounding-mode artefact of 81/288 = 28.125%).

The standard errors also match. `stata_probit` reports the *observed*
information (the Hessian of the log-likelihood), which is exactly what Stata's
`probit` default `vce(oim)` uses, so all eight probit SEs reproduce the printed
digits: `nwifeinc` .0048398, `educ` .0252542, `exper` .0187164, `expersq` .0006,
`age` .0084772, `kidslt6` .1185222 (Stata .1185223), `kidsge6` .0434768,
`_cons` .5085928 (Stata .508593) — the last two differ only in the eighth
significant digit, a display-rounding artefact. (Earlier releases used GLM's
IRLS *expected*-information variance, which for the non-canonical probit link
left every SE 1–2% high; that discrepancy is gone.) Logit SEs likewise agree,
as they always did — the logit link is canonical, so observed and expected
information coincide there.

## Example 15.3 (mroz.dta)

Smith–Blundell / control-function test for exogeneity of `educ` in the
participation probit (p. 474): the first-stage OLS residual is added as an
extra regressor and its t-ratio is the exogeneity test.

```{julia}
#=
reg educ nwifeinc exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6 motheduc fatheduc huseduc

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =     753
-------------+------------------------------           F(  9,   743) =   74.07
       Model |  1849.07781     9   205.45309           Prob > F      =  0.0000
    Residual |  2060.96203   743  2.77383853           R-squared     =  0.4729
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.4665
       Total |  3910.03984   752  5.19952106           Root MSE      =  1.6655

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        educ |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    nwifeinc |   .0156893   .0058267     2.69   0.007     .0042506     .027128
       exper |   .0577544   .0220604     2.62   0.009     .0144462    .1010625
     expersq |   -.000784    .000721    -1.09   0.277    -.0021994    .0006314
         age |  -.0059011   .0098709    -0.60   0.550    -.0252792     .013477
     kidslt6 |   .1195954   .1307071     0.91   0.360    -.1370038    .3761945
     kidsge6 |  -.0731404   .0515299    -1.42   0.156     -.174302    .0280212
    motheduc |   .1300347   .0225669     5.76   0.000     .0857322    .1743373
    fatheduc |   .0950702   .0214618     4.43   0.000     .0529373    .1372032
     huseduc |   .3475092   .0235063    14.78   0.000     .3013626    .3936558
       _cons |    5.43695   .5873755     9.26   0.000     4.283837    6.590064
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

predict u, res
probit inlf nwifeinc educ exper expersq age kidslt6 kidsge6 u

Probit estimates                                  Number of obs   =        753
                                                  LR chi2(8)      =     227.90
                                                  Prob > chi2     =     0.0000
Log likelihood = -400.92551                       Pseudo R2       =     0.2213

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
        inlf |      Coef.   Std. Err.      z    P>|z|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
    nwifeinc |  -.0102851   .0052347    -1.96   0.049     -.020545   -.0000253
        educ |   .1035752   .0403061     2.57   0.010     .0245767    .1825737
       exper |   .1262477   .0190256     6.64   0.000     .0889582    .1635373
     expersq |  -.0019432   .0006032    -3.22   0.001    -.0031254   -.0007609
         age |  -.0543808   .0086633    -6.28   0.000    -.0713605   -.0374012
     kidslt6 |  -.8630859   .1187394    -7.27   0.000    -1.095811    -.630361
     kidsge6 |   .0313802   .0437901     0.72   0.474    -.0544468    .1172071
           u |   .0433658    .050021     0.87   0.386    -.0546736    .1414051
       _cons |   .6209105   .6497413     0.96   0.339     -.652559     1.89438
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
=#
```

```{julia}
educ_fs = stata_regress(mroz; y = :educ,
                        x = [:nwifeinc, :exper, :expersq, :age, :kidslt6,
                             :kidsge6, :motheduc, :fatheduc, :huseduc])
```

```{julia}
mroz_cf = DataFrames.copy(mroz)
mroz_cf.u = FixedEffectModels.residuals(educ_fs, mroz)

pb_cf = stata_probit(mroz_cf,
    StatsModels.@formula(inlf ~ nwifeinc + educ + exper + expersq +
                                age + kidslt6 + kidsge6 + u))
```

**Comparison.** The first-stage regression reproduces Stata exactly (all ten
coefficients and SEs, `F(9,743) = 74.07`, `R-squared = 0.4729`,
`Root MSE = 1.6655`). The control-function probit reproduces every coefficient
to six or seven digits — in particular `u = .0433656` vs Stata's `.0433658`,
log-likelihood −400.9255 vs −400.92551, `LR chi2(8) = 227.90`, pseudo R²
0.2213. As in Example 15.2, the probit standard errors also reproduce Stata's
observed-information (`vce(oim)`) values to the printed digits — `nwifeinc`
.0052347, `educ` .0403061, `exper` .0190256, `expersq` .0006032, `age` .0086633,
`kidslt6` .1187393, `kidsge6` .04379, `u` .050021, `_cons` .6497409 — so the
z-statistics and p-values match as well (z on `u` = 0.87, p = 0.386, and `educ`
is not rejected as exogenous).

## Example 15.4 (keane.dta)

Multinomial logit for employment status in 1987 (p. 498).

```{julia}
#=
use keane, clear

* Stata 8 code.
mlogit status educ exper expersq black if year ==87, basecategory(1)

* Stata 9 code and output.
mlogit status educ exper expersq black if year ==87, baseoutcome(1)

Multinomial logistic regression                   Number of obs   =       1717
                                                  LR chi2(8)      =     583.72
                                                  Prob > chi2     =     0.0000
Log likelihood = -907.85723                       Pseudo R2       =     0.2433

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
      status |      Coef.   Std. Err.      z    P>|z|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
2            |
        educ |  -.6736313   .0698999    -9.64   0.000    -.8106325     -.53663
       exper |  -.1062149    .173282    -0.61   0.540    -.4458414    .2334116
     expersq |  -.0125152   .0252291    -0.50   0.620    -.0619633     .036933
       black |   .8130166   .3027231     2.69   0.007     .2196902    1.406343
       _cons |   10.27787   1.133336     9.07   0.000     8.056578    12.49917
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
3            |
        educ |  -.3146573   .0651096    -4.83   0.000    -.4422699   -.1870448
       exper |   .8487367   .1569856     5.41   0.000     .5410507    1.156423
     expersq |  -.0773003   .0229217    -3.37   0.001    -.1222261   -.0323746
       black |   .3113612   .2815339     1.11   0.269     -.240435    .8631574
       _cons |   5.543798   1.086409     5.10   0.000     3.414475    7.673121
------------------------------------------------------------------------------
(Outcome status==1 is the comparison group)

predict p1 p2 p3 if e(sample), p
(11006 missing values generated)

gen pstatus = .
(12723 missing values generated)

egen atest = rmax(p1 p2 p3)  if e(sample)
(11006 missing values generated)

foreach n of numlist 1/3 {
	replace pstatus = `n' if p`n'==atest & year ==87
		}
(36 real changes made)
(214 real changes made)
(1530 real changes made)

gen pdiff = (status == pstatus)
tab pdiff if year==87 & exper~=. & black ~=. & status ~=.

      pdiff |      Freq.     Percent        Cum.
------------+-----------------------------------
          0 |        351       20.44       20.44
          1 |      1,366       79.56      100.00
------------+-----------------------------------
      Total |      1,717      100.00

di 1366/1717
.79557368

test [2]: exper expersq

 ( 1)  [2]exper = 0
 ( 2)  [2]expersq = 0

           chi2(  2) =    6.12
         Prob > chi2 =    0.0468
=#
```

```{julia}
keane87 = DataFrames.dropmissing(
    keane[keane.year .== 87, [:status, :educ, :exper, :expersq, :black]])
DataFrames.nrow(keane87)
```

```{julia}
ml = stata_mlogit(keane87, :status, [:educ, :exper, :expersq, :black];
                  baseoutcome = 1)
```

### Predicted status and the hit rate

```{julia}
P    = StatEcon._c15_mnl_probs(ml.B, ml.X, ml.J)
pred = [argmax(view(P, i, :)) for i in 1:size(P, 1)]
ytru = Int.(ml.dfc.status)

n_hit = sum(pred .== ytru)
Printf.@printf("pdiff = 0 : %5d  %6.2f%%\n", length(ytru) - n_hit,
               100 * (length(ytru) - n_hit) / length(ytru))
Printf.@printf("pdiff = 1 : %5d  %6.2f%%\n", n_hit, 100 * n_hit / length(ytru))
Printf.@printf("Total     : %5d\n", length(ytru))
Printf.@printf("\ndi %d/%d = %.8f\n", n_hit, length(ytru), n_hit / length(ytru))
```

### `test [2]: exper expersq` — restricted to the *second* outcome only

`stata_test_mlogit` imposes the restriction across **all** outcomes, which is
Stata's `test exper expersq` (no equation prefix). The published example uses
the equation-specific form `test [2]: exper expersq`, so we build that
restriction matrix by hand from the fitted `B` / `V`.

```{julia}
K = size(ml.X, 2)                 # design has the intercept in column 1
row_of = Dict(v => i + 1 for (i, v) in enumerate(ml.regs))
# outcome "2" is the first non-base column of B
col2 = 1
R = zeros(2, K * (ml.J - 1))
R[1, (col2 - 1) * K + row_of[:exper]]   = 1.0
R[2, (col2 - 1) * K + row_of[:expersq]] = 1.0

b     = vec(ml.B)
chi2  = (R * b)' * LinearAlgebra.inv(R * ml.V * R') * (R * b)
pval  = 1 - Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Chisq(2), chi2)
println(" ( 1)  [2]exper = 0")
println(" ( 2)  [2]expersq = 0")
Printf.@printf("\n           chi2(  2) = %7.2f\n", chi2)
Printf.@printf("         Prob > chi2 = %7.4f\n", pval)
```

**Comparison.** Sample size (1,717), log-likelihood (−907.85723),
`LR chi2(8) = 583.72` and pseudo R² (0.2433) all match. Coefficients agree to
six decimals except `black` in the outcome-3 equation (.3112391 here vs
Stata's .3113612, a 4·10⁻⁴ relative difference) — an L-BFGS convergence-tolerance
difference in the last digits, not a specification difference; its z and p are
unchanged (1.11 / 0.269). Standard errors agree to five significant digits
(the FD-Hessian OIM vs Stata's analytic one). The hit rate is exactly
1366/1717 = **.79557368**, and the equation-2 Wald test gives
`chi2(2) = 6.12`, `Prob > chi2 = 0.0468`, matching Stata.

## Example 15.5 (pension.dta)

Ordered probit for the percentage of pension assets held in stocks (p. 507).

```{julia}
#=
use pension, clear

reg pctstck choice age educ female black married finc25 finc35 finc50 finc75 finc100 ///
	finc101 wealth89 prftshr

      Source |       SS       df       MS              Number of obs =     194
-------------+------------------------------           F( 14,   179) =    1.42
       Model |  30402.0516    14  2171.57511           Prob > F      =  0.1486
    Residual |  274134.031   179  1531.47503           R-squared     =  0.0998
-------------+------------------------------           Adj R-squared =  0.0294
       Total |  304536.082   193  1577.90716           Root MSE      =  39.134

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     pctstck |      Coef.   Std. Err.      t    P>|t|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
      choice |   12.04773   6.298171     1.91   0.057    -.3804881    24.47594
         age |  -1.625967   .7748246    -2.10   0.037    -3.154932   -.0970012
        educ |   .7538685   1.207392     0.62   0.533    -1.628684    3.136421
      female |   1.302856   7.163775     0.18   0.856    -12.83346    15.43917
       black |   3.967391   9.782799     0.41   0.686    -15.33706    23.27184
     married |   3.303436   7.997618     0.41   0.680    -12.47831    19.08518
      finc25 |  -18.18567   14.12026    -1.29   0.199    -46.04924    9.677906
      finc35 |  -3.925374   14.48565    -0.27   0.787    -32.50999    24.65924
      finc50 |  -8.128784   14.34191    -0.57   0.572    -36.42976    20.17219
      finc75 |  -17.57921   16.07766    -1.09   0.276    -49.30534    14.14693
     finc100 |   -6.74559   15.79116    -0.43   0.670    -37.90637    24.41519
     finc101 |  -28.34407    17.9049    -1.58   0.115    -63.67591    6.987774
    wealth89 |  -.0026918   .0124603    -0.22   0.829    -.0272797    .0218961
     prftshr |   15.80791   7.332677     2.16   0.032     1.338299    30.27752
       _cons |   134.1161   55.70525     2.41   0.017      24.1926    244.0395
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

oprobit pctstck choice age educ female black married finc25 finc35 finc50 finc75 finc100 finc101 ///
	wealth89 prftshr

Ordered probit estimates                          Number of obs   =        194
                                                  LR chi2(14)     =      20.77
                                                  Prob > chi2     =     0.1077
Log likelihood =  -201.9865                       Pseudo R2       =     0.0489

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
     pctstck |      Coef.   Std. Err.      z    P>|z|     [95% Conf. Interval]
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
      choice |    .371171   .1841121     2.02   0.044      .010318    .7320241
         age |  -.0500516   .0226063    -2.21   0.027    -.0943591    -.005744
        educ |   .0261382   .0352561     0.74   0.458    -.0429626    .0952389
      female |   .0455642    .206004     0.22   0.825    -.3581963    .4493246
       black |   .0933923   .2820403     0.33   0.741    -.4593965    .6461811
     married |   .0935981   .2332114     0.40   0.688    -.3634878     .550684
      finc25 |  -.5784299    .423162    -1.37   0.172    -1.407812    .2509524
      finc35 |  -.1346721   .4305242    -0.31   0.754    -.9784841    .7091399
      finc50 |  -.2620401   .4265936    -0.61   0.539    -1.098148    .5740681
      finc75 |  -.5662312   .4780035    -1.18   0.236    -1.503101    .3706385
     finc100 |  -.2278963   .4685942    -0.49   0.627    -1.146324    .6905316
     finc101 |  -.8641109   .5291111    -1.63   0.102     -1.90115    .1729279
    wealth89 |  -.0000956   .0003737    -0.26   0.798    -.0008279    .0006368
     prftshr |   .4817182   .2161233     2.23   0.026     .0581243     .905312
-------------+----------------------------------------------------------------
       _cut1 |  -3.087373   1.623765          (Ancillary parameters)
       _cut2 |  -2.053553   1.618611
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

gen pclass=.
(226 missing values generated)

predict c1 if e(sample), outcome(0)
(option p assumed; predicted probability)
(32 missing values generated)

predict c2 if e(sample), outcome(50)
(option p assumed; predicted probability)
(32 missing values generated)

predict c3 if e(sample), outcome(100)
(option p assumed; predicted probability)
(32 missing values generated)

egen atest = rmax(c1 c2 c3)
(32 missing values generated)

foreach n of numlist 1/3 {
	replace pclass = `n' if c`n' ==atest
		}
(97 real changes made)
(113 real changes made)
(80 real changes made)

tab pclass pctstck if e(sample)

           | 0=mstbnds,50=mixed,100=mststcks
    pclass |         0         50        100 |     Total
-----------+---------------------------------+----------
         1 |        33         21         11 |        65
         2 |        25         31         25 |        81
         3 |         6         20         22 |        48
-----------+---------------------------------+----------
     Total |        64         72         58 |       194

di (33+31+22)/194
.44329897

di 33/64
.515625

di 31/72
.43055556

di 22/58
.37931034
=#
```

### OLS benchmark

```{julia}
XP = [:choice, :age, :educ, :female, :black, :married, :finc25, :finc35,
      :finc50, :finc75, :finc100, :finc101, :wealth89, :prftshr]
pen_ols = stata_regress(pension; y = :pctstck, x = XP)
```

### Ordered probit

`StatEcon`'s `stata_oprobit` fits the ordered-probit MLE (Stata `oprobit`),
using the same monotone cut-point parameterisation (τ₁ = θ₁, τⱼ = τⱼ₋₁ + exp θⱼ)
and a finite-difference observed-information VCE with a delta-method correction
back to the τ scale — i.e. exactly Stata's `vce(oim)`. It is `stata_ologit` with
the logistic link replaced by the standard-normal CDF.

```{julia}
op = stata_oprobit(pension, :pctstck, XP)   # StatEcon ships this now

# variables the classification table below reuses
β̂, τ̂ = op.β, op.τ
Xop   = op.X
Φ(z)  = Distributions.cdf(Distributions.Normal(), z)
pen_est = DataFrames.dropmissing(pension[:, vcat(:pctstck, XP)])
cats  = sort(unique(Float64.(pen_est.pctstck)))
y_idx = [findfirst(==(v), cats) for v in Float64.(pen_est.pctstck)]
N     = length(y_idx)
```

### Predicted class vs. actual (`tab pclass pctstck`)

```{julia}
η   = Xop * β̂
Pop = hcat(Φ.(τ̂[1] .- η),
           Φ.(τ̂[2] .- η) .- Φ.(τ̂[1] .- η),
           1 .- Φ.(τ̂[2] .- η))
pclass = [argmax(view(Pop, i, :)) for i in 1:N]

ctab = [sum((pclass .== a) .& (y_idx .== b)) for a in 1:3, b in 1:3]
Printf.@printf("    pclass |%9d %9d %9d |    Total\n", 0, 50, 100)
for a in 1:3
    Printf.@printf("%10d |%9d %9d %9d | %8d\n", a,
                   ctab[a, 1], ctab[a, 2], ctab[a, 3], sum(ctab[a, :]))
end
Printf.@printf("     Total |%9d %9d %9d | %8d\n",
               sum(ctab[:, 1]), sum(ctab[:, 2]), sum(ctab[:, 3]), sum(ctab))
Printf.@printf("\ndi (%d+%d+%d)/%d = %.8f\n", ctab[1,1], ctab[2,2], ctab[3,3], N,
               (ctab[1,1] + ctab[2,2] + ctab[3,3]) / N)
Printf.@printf("di %d/%d = %.8f\n", ctab[1,1], sum(ctab[:,1]), ctab[1,1]/sum(ctab[:,1]))
Printf.@printf("di %d/%d = %.8f\n", ctab[2,2], sum(ctab[:,2]), ctab[2,2]/sum(ctab[:,2]))
Printf.@printf("di %d/%d = %.8f\n", ctab[3,3], sum(ctab[:,3]), ctab[3,3]/sum(ctab[:,3]))
```

**Comparison.** The OLS regression matches Stata to all printed digits
(`F(14,179) = 1.42`, `R-squared = 0.0998`, `Root MSE = 39.134`, and every
coefficient/SE). `stata_oprobit` reproduces the published
`oprobit` output exactly: all fourteen coefficients and standard errors, both
cut-points (−3.087373 / 1.623765 and −2.053553 / 1.618611), the log-likelihood
−201.9865, `LR chi2(14) = 20.77` and pseudo R² 0.0489. The classification table
is identical (33/21/11, 25/31/25, 6/20/22) and so are the four displayed ratios:
.44329897, .515625, .43055556, .37931034.

Note: use `stata_oprobit` here rather than `stata_ologit` — the published table
is `oprobit`, and `stata_ologit` fits the ordered **logit** link. (`stata_ologit`
works fine on three-or-more-category outcomes and reports a genuine
log-likelihood, `LR chi2` and pseudo R² — earlier releases had a cut-point
warm-start `DomainError` and zeroed LR/pseudo-R² statistics, both since fixed.)
